objects.name: VA3IAN
objects.description: Reporting analyst (Ian, VA3IAN). Identity object used as created_by_ref for objects in this bundle.

objects.name: ShinyHunters-branded sextortion campaign
objects.description: Mass-mailed sextortion campaign impersonating the ShinyHunters threat group. The operator is unrelated to the real ShinyHunters data-theft collective; the brand is borrowed for menace and credibility. Lure references a CarGurus.com database breach as the alleged source of the recipient's email, claims device-wide compromise (microphone, camera, keyboard, photos, contacts, browsing history), and threatens release of fabricated explicit recordings unless USD 2,000 in Bitcoin is paid within 48 hours.
objects.objective: Coerce recipients into paying ~USD 2,000 in Bitcoin via fabricated claims of device compromise and explicit-video recording.

objects.name: Unknown sextortion operator (ShinyHunters impersonator)
objects.description: Unidentified operator running a templated sextortion campaign. Impersonates the ShinyHunters data-theft group for psychological pressure. Capability profile is low: no evidence of actual device compromise, no proof artefacts presented to victims, reuses a publicly documented lure template dating to 2018.

objects.name: ShinyHunters (real group, referenced for context only)
objects.description: Reference object only. Real ShinyHunters is an established data-theft and extortion collective active since 2020, associated with breaches including Wattpad, Tokopedia, AT&T, Ticketmaster and the broader 2024 Snowflake-tenant campaign (overlap with UNC5537 / Scattered Spider clusters). This actor targets enterprises and does NOT conduct individual sextortion. Included to disambiguate from the impersonator above.

objects.name: Phishing - Sextortion
objects.description: Mass-emailed sextortion lure claiming device compromise and explicit-video recording, demanding cryptocurrency payment. No technical compromise occurs; the threat is purely psychological coercion.

objects.name: Impersonation of named threat group
objects.description: Operator borrows the name of a well-known threat group (here: ShinyHunters) to add credibility and menace to a low-sophistication lure. Common indicator of social-engineering-only operations with no underlying technical capability.

objects.name: Breach-pretext lure (CarGurus reference)
objects.description: Lure cites a real-world breach or combolist source (here: CarGurus.com) to explain how the operator allegedly obtained the recipient's email. The breach reference is plausible because the recipient's email may genuinely appear in third-party combolists, lending false credibility to the rest of the claim.

objects.name: Claimed device exploit (fabricated)
objects.description: The lure claims an unspecified exploit was installed across all victim devices granting access to microphone, camera, keyboard, photos, browsing history, conversations and contacts. No malware exists; the claim is fabricated. Captured here as a malware object only to model what the lure asserts, not to assert real presence on any system.

objects.name: BTC wallet - sextortion ransom drop
objects.description: Bitcoin (bech32 / native SegWit) address used as the ransom drop in the ShinyHunters-branded sextortion campaign. Demand: USD 2,000 within 48 hours.

objects.name: Email lure phrase - ShinyHunters sextortion template
objects.description: Distinctive phrase from the lure body. Useful as a content-based detection / search pivot for similar messages across mail gateways.
objects.pattern: [email-message:body MATCHES 'We have your photos, browsing history, conversations, and contact list']

objects.name: Email lure - opening greeting plus brand claim
objects.description: Opening line pattern from the lure. Combined with the BTC wallet and breach pretext, supports confident attribution to the same campaign template.
objects.pattern: [email-message:body MATCHES 'We are the ShinyHunters hacking group']

objects.name: Email lure - CarGurus breach pretext
objects.description: Specific breach-pretext phrase used to explain how the operator allegedly obtained the recipient's email.
objects.pattern: [email-message:body MATCHES 'We gained access to the Cargurus.com database']

objects.description: Campaign is attributed to the unidentified sextortion operator.
Sextortion operator borrows the ShinyHunters name to add menace; the two are operationally unrelated.
Campaign uses sextortion phishing technique (T1566).
Campaign uses named-group impersonation for credibility.
Campaign uses CarGurus breach-pretext to justify possession of the recipient's email.
BTC wallet observed as the ransom drop indicates this campaign.
Lure phrase indicator indicates this campaign.
Opening-line indicator indicates this campaign.
CarGurus breach-pretext indicator indicates this campaign.

objects.name: ShinyHunters-branded sextortion campaign — observed sample
objects.description: Recipient (VA3IAN) received a sextortion email on 2026-05-20 matching the publicly documented ShinyHunters-branded sextortion template. Lure pretexts CarGurus.com breach, claims full device compromise without evidence, and demands USD 2,000 in Bitcoin to wallet bc1q6k7j2crsjalh90a78jzw4wqxstfzvyycxhdyrx within 48 hours. Recipient has not paid and has not been compromised. This bundle is for tracking and detection-engineering use; the operator is unrelated to the real ShinyHunters group.

external_references.description: Public writeup describing the same template, wallet, and ransom amount
